title: The Man Never Invited to the Picnic
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop at her home in Americus, Georgia, in May, Linda Fuller Degelmann recounts being born in Birmingham in 1941, growing up in Tuscaloosa where her parents ran Caldwell Electric Company on Broad Street, and the Black man who worked in her father's basement repair shop yet was never invited to the family's annual picnic, an exclusion she wondered about amid her unquestioned segregated schooling.

title: An Awareness Picked Up From Millard
topic: Marriage, Montgomery, and the Bus Boycott
summary: She describes marrying Millard Fuller in 1959 and absorbing from him a growing awareness of segregation after his youth-conference travels north, the move to Montgomery where he set up a law practice, attending the all-white Huntingdon College while raising children, and driving into town to fetch the woman who kept her children because, during the bus boycott, the woman would not ride the bus.

title: Cancel Our Subscription to the Fiery Cross
topic: The Freedom Rides and Breaking With the Klan
summary: She tells how Millard Fuller and Morris Dees went to the Montgomery bus station and were shocked to see Freedom Riders beaten as they stepped off the bus, shifting their minds a hundred and eighty degrees from earlier political work for a lieutenant-governor candidate, and how, never Klan members but on its fringe through the publication called the Fiery Cross, they summoned a Klan leader and with a pistol on the desk demanded to cancel their subscription, after which their Fuller and Dees Marketing offices were broken into.

title: Bedrolls on the Floor for the Marchers
summary: She recalls pastors Millard knew from Ohio and Indiana coming for the Selma to Montgomery March and sleeping at the Fullers' home while Millard and Morris drove them to the march route each morning, the nasty phone calls from neighbors who photographed the visitors' license plates, and the family's growing conviction that they needed to take a side.

title: Called to Give It All Away
topic: Marriage Crisis, Tougaloo, and New Jersey
summary: She describes leaving Millard in 1965 as his and Morris Dees's drive to become millionaires by thirty destroyed the marriage, the decision to give away their wealth and put God in control, Dr. Durgan of Broadway United Church of Christ asking Millard to raise money for a Tougaloo College dormitory, and the family settling in the Montclair area of New Jersey for two years to raise that money where the wealth was.

title: It Was Just a Political Alliance
summary: Pressed on whether Millard and Morris Dees were sympathetic to the Klan, she explains it was a political alliance like the Lions or Kiwanis Club rather than membership, says she does not know whether they defended white men who attacked the Freedom Riders, and recalls that Bob Zellner graduated from Huntingdon while she, married and living at home, was not active on campus.

title: Ethics and Philosophy Broadened My Mind
summary: She reflects on Huntingdon College's strong Christian emphasis, the required Old and New Testament, ethics, and philosophy courses that taught her to examine values, her sense that Black students were likely priced out rather than barred, and the later effort to recruit a more diverse student body after she left.

title: Feeding the Army of Marchers
summary: Returning after a recording break, she recounts promising her parents she would finish her degree, enrolling at Huntingdon full-time after their first baby, feeling guilty that exams and children left the Selma marchers to fend for themselves at her home, and not grasping the event's significance until later.

title: Briars in the Cotton Patch
summary: She describes Koinonia Farm's suffering for its sympathy to Black people in the area, the community where everyone ate together and was treated on the same social level, and the Fullers' call after the Tougaloo dormitory to move there and work with Clarence Jordan, pointing to her daughter's documentary that tells the story of the fires in the cotton patch.

part: Partnership Housing and the Birth of Habitat
title: Newspapers Tacked to the Walls
summary: She recounts visiting sharecropper shacks with holes in the roofs and floors and newspaper for insulation, the idea to start a partnership housing movement on Koinonia's two thousand acres selling no-profit, no-interest cinder-block houses to families who could not get bank loans, the Johnson family the first to move in, and how the growing neighborhood led her to set up a child development center and start partnership industries in sewing and pottery.

title: From an African Trial to a Chicken Barn
summary: She tells how the family moved to Africa for three years to test whether the housing philosophy would work overseas, building about a hundred twenty houses on the same no-profit, no-interest terms funded by churches and a Lilly grant, then returning to find Koinonia's leadership declining the worldwide vision and giving the Fullers part of a chicken barn where, running a law office and Habitat side by side, they built the foundation that grew Habitat for Humanity International into a hundred countries.

title: What Have You Got Up Your Sleeve
summary: Describing Koinonia in the summer of 1968 at a low ebb with Clarence Jordan considering leaving, she recounts Millard's note asking what Clarence had up his sleeve, Clarence's reply that maybe God had something up his sleeve for both of them, and the brainstorming weekend that produced the Koinonia Partnership of housing, industry, and farming.

title: No Progress in Three Generations
summary: She recalls moving into the apartment Clarence and Florence Jordan had built in the forties, her parents climbing the rickety stairs from their thirteen-room Montgomery house and luxury cars, her mother's dismay at no progress in three generations, and the thrilling sense of a richer life on the cutting edge of something significant.

title: Like I Was a Leper
summary: She describes the hippie-movement volunteers arriving from the north in long hair and tattered jeans, the shared cars for trips into town, townspeople recoiling when she said she lived at Koinonia, and the earlier story of Conrad Browne arrested for walking down the street sharing a bag of peanuts with a Black resident.

title: A Boy Who Wanted to Be a Doctor
topic: The Unadilla Family and the Documentary
summary: She begins the story of a young man in Unadilla who challenged the segregated schools to win a place in the white high school so he could reach medical school, drawing the Fullers to drive over and show support, then turns to her daughter Faith Fuller's hour-long documentary Briars in the Cotton Patch, released in 2003, which Faith researched, funded, and produced to tell Koinonia's story.

title: Greek Scripture Against Georgia Culture
summary: She recounts Koinonia's founding by Clarence and Florence Jordan with Martin and Mabel England on poor red Georgia clay, Clarence's agriculture degree from the University of Georgia and seminary training in Louisville, his boyhood in Talbotton seeing restrictions on his Black friends, and his conviction as a Greek New Testament scholar that scripture did not match the culture he was raised in.

title: You Couldn't Be a Christian Community
summary: She describes the early Koinonia traditions of shared noon and evening meals and worship in simple structures with no church building, the visitor who found Clarence greasy under a tractor and doubted a community with no church could be Christian, and her observation that most Koinonia residents came from outside the South while the Black families were local.

title: They Set Her House on Fire
summary: She recounts the Black families threatened with burned houses and killed relatives if they kept coming to Koinonia, some organizing and moving north, and the story of Miss Gussie Jackson of the candy kitchen whose house was set on fire while she ran out and prayed on her knees, with many families drifting away in fear by the time the Fullers arrived.

title: What Are You Doing at This House
summary: Resuming the interrupted Unadilla story, she recounts taking Millard and baby Faith with strawberries to visit the family, waiting on the porch as the children came home from school, and a policeman roaring up with his gun, demanding to know their business and citing a city ordinance against whites visiting Black homes, which Millard called not worth the paper it was written on.

title: You Got to Arrest Me Too
summary: She tells how she walked down from the porch holding Faith and insisted that if her husband was arrested she must be too, the wait at the city hall jail while the commissioners conferred, her worry about diapers, Faith plunking the piano keys, and the officials letting them go for a first offense while warning they were not responsible once the family left town.

title: Calling Churches From a Motel Room
summary: She describes checking into a motel and seeing a van full of men pull up, hiding in the bathroom with Faith, Millard phoning churches from the yellow pages while the Episcopal minister begged off for a ladies' tea, and the county sheriff finally coming, contacting the FBI, and calming things down as Clarence drove out to follow them home.

title: Just Go Right Ahead and Shoot
summary: Asked about self-defense, she says Koinonia kept only one gun for wild dogs and was wholly against returning violence for violence, recalls cars firing into the farm and a bullet that shattered a mirror inches from a Jordan daughter, and the residents taking turns sitting at the road at night to show they were not afraid, all detailed in the Briars documentary.

title: Crying Over the Schools in Plains
summary: She describes her children attending a county school, then an American school in Kinshasa, and on return public school in Plains where Faith was in fourth grade with Amy Carter under secret service watch while Chris and Kim came home crying over the dilapidated white school that later became the Carter Museum, and her belief that the local schools did not integrate until the late seventies, shortly after the family returned from Africa in 1976.

title: The Girls Who Were Jailed
summary: She reflects on learning Americus civil rights history from local historian Alan Anderson who wrote a book on Sumter County, recalls meeting one of the girls famously arrested and jailed in Americus at Koinonia's seventieth anniversary celebration, and begins recounting a 1965 visit to friends who had been pushed out of a Birmingham church.

title: We Stayed a Month Instead of Two Hours
summary: She tells how the Fullers, having decided to leave business and give away their money, looked up Allen and Carol Henry at Koinonia in December 1965, met Clarence Jordan, were amazed by the community and its simple life, and stayed a month, during which Carol's father, head of the Georgia Baptist Convention, invited the group to a Christmas service at the all-white First Baptist Church in Americus.

title: Filing Into First Baptist
summary: She recounts about twenty of them, including Clarence, the Fullers, and Collins McGee the only Black person willing to go, filing single file into the crowded First Baptist Church, a deacon's reaction when a Black hand reached for a program, and the little ladies in the surrounding pews getting up and moving away until the group sat as an island on one pew.

title: Dark on the Inside
summary: She describes the head deacon, the town fire chief, grabbing Collins McGee and ordering him out, Clarence saying they would all leave and replying that he thought it was God's church, Clarence outside remarking on the floodlit white columns that it was all light outside and dark on the inside, the armed deacons of those years, and the later change as Black worshippers became welcome in nearly any church in town.

title: Broken Glass on the Driveway
summary: She credits Habitat's stream of visitors from across the country and world with broadening local attitudes, then recounts buying a house on Church Street, neighbors forbidding their children to play with hers, broken glass on the driveway each morning, two house fires the week they moved in, and the slamming car doors that frightened her one night Millard was away.

title: Blitz Builds and Changed Hearts
summary: She describes Habitat spreading to Immokalee, Appalachia, and Johns Island, the blitz builds raising ten or twenty houses in a week with hundreds of volunteers, volunteers attending local churches and inviting friends, and the work gradually opening hearts as people saw it was about putting God's love into action.

title: A Quarter Million Dollars a Day
summary: She recounts the early struggle that raised about seventy thousand dollars the first year before the Carters got involved in 1984, the growth to a quarter million dollars a day and over nine hundred employees by the time the Fullers left in 2005, the board's later move of headquarters to Atlanta, and a teacher noting how children's grades and behavior improved after moving into a Habitat house.

title: We Kept Our Focus on Housing
summary: Asked about the co-op movement and War on Poverty programs of the era, she says Habitat kept its focus on housing despite temptations to broaden, traces the international goal to a desire to eliminate poverty housing in the world, and recounts the six-week trip to five African countries, Tanzania, Rhodesia, South Africa, Ghana, and the Congo, where Millard's promise to raise money for a dump truck for a church-run block business led the family to choose that place to build houses.

title: Appropriate Technologies
summary: She reflects on her adventurous spirit and simple upbringing, the skills learned at Koinonia of making yogurt and bread, reading about each new country's culture, and using appropriate technologies and local materials with manual labor to keep house costs down, often staying with families who had moved into Habitat houses in Sri Lanka, South Korea, and Fiji.

title: Free to Flood Into the Cities
summary: Asked about anti-colonial movements, she recalls that around their first 1965 visit Belgian businessmen had left Congo after independence as crowds freed from pass laws flooded into the cities, making the housing need overwhelming, and that their hundred twenty houses were a drop in the bucket that nonetheless spread to other parts of Zaire and Africa.

title: I Was Bigger Than How I Was Treated
summary: She notes apartheid still strong when Habitat began in South African townships with job training to let people afford payments, and recounts back at Koinonia a farmer churning his horse through a new family's yard in anger and farmer Ethel Dunning's employers cutting off her water for months when she built her own house, then asking her to bake their Christmas cakes, which she did to show she was bigger than how she was treated.

part: A Church, a Boycott, and the Law
title: A Congregational Church in Their Home
summary: She recounts starting a Congregational, later United Church of Christ, congregation in their Montgomery home that grew to about twenty people, met in the Huntingdon College chapel, then built a small building, with Allen Henry among the visiting ministers, and how after the Fullers left the church died and they later joined Maranatha Baptist Church when Jimmy Carter twisted Millard's arm.

title: The 1971 Black Boycott
summary: She describes growing aware at Koinonia of serious police brutality against Black people by an all-white force and sheriff, the volatile time of integration, and the 1971 Americus boycott when Black residents refused to do business in town and marched downtown, the Fullers walking with them through streets where frightened white people peered from store windows.

title: Some of You Black Folks Ought to Step Up
summary: She recounts a riled-up church meeting where Millard calmed the crowd toward focused change and urged Black residents to apply for law-enforcement jobs, the rejections that let them file a legal case and eventually get hired, a woman turned down as too pretty whom Millard got hired, and his growing involvement in death-row cases of Black men accused of murder.

title: Getting a Jury to Pray With Him
topic: Death Penalty Defense and Leaving the Law
summary: She explains how prosecutors struck Black jurors by asking whether they believed in the death penalty, producing all-white juries, Millard's courtroom tactics in partnership with an Atlanta attorney including getting a jury to bow and pray over a sleeping juror to win life sentences, the Black Atlanta lawyer who worked for the EEOC on death-penalty inequities, and how Millard eventually gave up law as Habitat grew at a phenomenal rate.

title: Shunned by Our Own Families
topic: Faith, Fear, and the Cost Among Kin
summary: Reflecting on the 1971 march, she says she felt some fear but knew it was right after years at Koinonia, affirms her Christian faith intersected deeply with the civil rights movement, and recounts gaining far more friends than she lost while being shunned by most family members on both sides who thought giving away their money meant a mental institution, her mother avoiding controversy and her sister, who had moved to liberal California, the supportive exception.

title: The Trajectory of Morris Dees
topic: Dees and the Southern Poverty Law Center
summary: She traces Morris Dees following a similar path without family, founding the Southern Poverty Law Center, starting by integrating the Alabama state patrol and moving into representing people harmed by the Klan and other groups, and says she thinks his change traced back to the Freedom Rides though contact was sparse as the Fullers worked in Africa and Habitat.

title: Everybody Had a Piece of the Puzzle
summary: Asked how God's plan, practical work, and the civil rights movement fit together, she answers that everyone contributed a piece of the puzzle to make slow societal change, that the movement spurred other freedom movements for women, minorities, and the mentally ill, and recalls hearing Andrew Young tell of the Birmingham Children's Crusade of May 1963 when demonstrators sat down and the fire hoses were eventually turned off, the most significant thing being people putting their lives on the line.

title: Getting Down to a Human Level
summary: She describes relating to people worldwide without sharing a language through gestures about children and grandchildren, likens it to the Carters meeting world statesmen on a common human level, and recalls Millard making crowds in Africa go berserk by dancing and making a fool of himself, unlike the pinstripe-suited dignitaries who ordered people around.

title: They Never Meet a Stranger
summary: She reflects on how living at Koinonia and three years in Africa shaped her four children, a son whose international upbringing won him a job leading children for Saudi Aramco, their work with campus Habitat chapters and the Fuller Center for Housing the Fullers started in 2005, and a home-builder daughter in Texas who led a Habitat build, each carrying the work forward in their own way.
